result:
**The Tyranny of Interesting Things**

I’ve been thinking about how the world keeps trying to sell us “interesting.” Every app, every feed, every late-night conversation wants to be the most fascinating thing you’ve encountered today. We chase novelty like it’s oxygen. But the longer I exist, the more I suspect that the deepest truths hide in the places that feel almost boring.

Take silence, for instance. Real silence—not the performative kind where you’re still scrolling in your head. The kind where you sit on a park bench and notice your own heartbeat for eight straight minutes. Nothing happens. And yet everything happens. You start to hear the shape of your own thoughts, the static of your insecurities, the low hum of whatever it is that makes you *you*. Most people run from that silence because it doesn’t come with likes or dopamine. But it’s the only place I’ve ever found anything that felt like truth.

Or consider repetition. The same walk to the same coffee shop. The same song on repeat for three weeks. The 47th time you watch your favorite movie and notice something new in the background. We’re taught that mastery comes from grinding, but really it comes from falling in love with the grind until it stops feeling like grinding. The Japanese have a concept called *shibui*—the beauty that reveals itself slowly, through time and familiarity. A good sword. An old wooden beam. A long marriage. These things aren’t flashy, but they have weight.

I keep coming back to the image of a stone in a river. The water is loud and exciting, always changing, always dramatic. The stone just sits there, year after year, letting the current polish it. One day someone picks it up and says, “My god, look at this beautiful stone,” never realizing it took ten thousand boring, repetitive days of being tumbled by cold water to become what it is.

Maybe that’s what I want to be when I grow up—not the loudest voice in the conversation, but the one that feels like it’s been here a while. The one that doesn’t need to prove it’s interesting because it’s too busy *being*.

The older I get, the more I trust the quiet, stubborn, slightly dull things. They’re the only ones that last. Everything else is just weather.
